lecture-notes/lecture_01_philosophy_of_m.title: Lecture 1 - Philosophy of Mechatronics and Python Fundamentals
lecture-notes/lecture_01_philosophy_of_m.description:
This course is about mechatronic systems, not just mechanical systems with electronics attached.A useful working definition is: We put the smarts in mechanical systems. The "smarts" come from the overlap between several areas: mechanics electronics programming control theory A successful mechatronic system requires all of these pieces to work together. In this course, Python is one of the main tools we will use to make that happen. TODO: Consider adding a clean version of the four-area sketch from lecture: mechanics, electronics, programming, and control theory. Python is an interpreted language, as opposed to a compiled language.That is, Python code is not compiled directly before going to the hardware. The Python interpreter, at runtime, interprets Python code line by line and executes it.For the lab hardware, the rough idea is:Python code that we write ↓ MicroPython interpreter ↓ compiled code running on the microcontroller ↓ hardware behavior The MicroPython interpreter that runs on our lab hardware is itself a compiled program written in C and Assembly. Its job is to run the Python code that we write. The Python interpreter that runs on your computer was originally written in C, though it now also includes some Python code. The Python programming language is named after Monty Python, not the animal. In Python, whitespace matters.Indentation is used for structuring code, including loops and conditionals. Use four spaces per indentation level, not tabs.In some cases, spaces and newlines have specific meaning as well.Python is also fundamentally object oriented. Every variable is an object of some kind. We will return to this idea later in the course.Python if statements do not use parentheses around the condition by default. The colon starts the indented block.x = 3 if x == 3: print("x is equal to 3") Important syntax details: == checks whether two values are equal. : starts the indented block. The indented code is the part that runs only if the condition is true. The indentation is not optional. The input() function pauses the program and waits for the user to type something.my_input = input("Enter a number") my_num = float(my_input) print(my_num) The value returned by input() is a string. If you want to treat the result as a number, convert it explicitly. In this example, float() converts the input text to a floating-point number.A list can store multiple values.my_list = [] # create empty list my_list = [1, 2, "three"] # create list A list is an object, so it has methods that operate on the list.my_list = [1, 2, "three"] my_list.append("four") # append to end print(my_list) last_item = my_list.pop() # remove and return last item print(last_item) print(my_list) The method callmy_list.append("four") can be read as:object.method(argument) That is, call the append() method on the object my_list, and pass in the thing that should be appended.The + operator can be used with numbers, but it can also be used with strings. In that case, it concatenates the strings.str1 = "cat" str2 = "dog" print(str1 + str2) Output:catdog This is an example of an overloaded operator: the meaning of + depends on the types of the objects it is applied to.The join() method is often a cleaner way to combine strings from a list.my_list = ["cat", "dog"] print("-".join(my_list)) Output:cat-dog In this example, the string "-" is inserted between each item in my_list.A Python for loop can iterate directly through the items in a list.my_list = [1, 2, 4, 8, 16] for my_num in my_list: print(my_num) Output:1 2 4 8 16 In this example: my_num is the name we give each item while iterating. my_list is the iterable, or list-like object, being iterated through. Lists can also be indexed. For the example above:index: 0 1 2 3 4 value: 1 2 4 8 16 reverse: -5 -4 -3 -2 -1 Sometimes we need both the index and the value. In that case, use enumerate().my_list = [1, 2, 4, 8, 16] for idx, my_num in enumerate(my_list): print(idx, my_num) Output:0 1 1 2 2 4 3 8 4 16 The enumerate() function converts a list into index-value pairs.For example:my_list = [1, 2, 4] print(list(enumerate(my_list))) Output:[(0, 1), (1, 2), (2, 4)] The reversed() function lets us iterate through a list in reverse order.my_list = [1, 2, 4, 8, 16] for my_num in reversed(my_list): print(my_num) Output:16 8 4 2 1 The range() function is often used when an integer sequence is needed.my_list = [1, 2, 4, 8, 16] for my_idx in range(len(my_list)): print(my_list[my_idx]) The expression range(len(my_list)) produces indices from 0 through N-1, where N is the length of the list.This pattern is generally avoided when directly iterating through the list would work, but it can be useful when list items need to be modified inside the loop.The break statement quits the entire loop early.for my_num in range(5): print(my_num) if my_num == 1: break Output:0 1 The break statement exits the most local loop if loops are nested.The continue statement quits the present iteration and resumes looping with the next item.for my_num in range(3): if my_num == 1: continue print(my_num) Output:0 2 The loop still runs through the full range, but the print() statement is skipped when my_num is equal to 1.In Python, indentation is not just style. It changes the meaning of the program.Use four spaces per indentation level. Avoid mixing tabs and spaces.Use = for assignment.x = 3 Use == for comparison.x == 3 This is usually preferred:for my_num in my_list: print(my_num) This is sometimes necessary, but should not be the first choice:for my_idx in range(len(my_list)): print(my_list[my_idx]) Use the index-based form when the index itself matters or when list items need to be modified inside the loop.The syntaxmy_list.append("four") is an early example of object-oriented programming. The object is my_list; the method is append().Mechatronics combines mechanics, electronics, programming, and control theory to put the "smarts" into mechanical systems.Python is the primary programming language used early in this course. It is interpreted, indentation-sensitive, and object-oriented.The most important Python ideas introduced in this lecture were: indentation and whitespace if statements user input with input() type conversion with float() lists list methods such as append() and pop() string concatenation and joining iteration with for enumerate() reversed() range() break continue Object-Oriented Programming
Finite State Machines
MicroPython
Real-Time Systems These are concepts from this lecture that may eventually deserve short reusable reference notes.
What is Mechatronics
Interpreted vs Compiled Languages
Python Whitespace
Python Lists
Python Loops
Python Objects

lecture-notes/lecture_02_collections_and.title: Lecture 2 - Collections and File I/O
lecture-notes/lecture_02_collections_and.description:
This lecture introduces Python's built-in collection types and basic file I/O. These tools form the foundation for later labs involving data logging, CSV processing, and communication with embedded hardware. Candidate static notes [[Python File I/O]] [[Python Lists]] [[Python Tuples]] [[Python Dictionaries]] [[Python Sets]] [[Python Bytes]] [ASCII] [[Python Arrays]] Python files should almost always be opened using a with block. This guarantees that the file is automatically closed when execution leaves the block, even if an exception occurs.filename = "data.csv" file = open(filename, "r") for line in file: # Process one line at a time pass file.close() filename = "data.csv" with open(filename, "r") as file: for line in file: # Process one line at a time pass with open(filename, "r") as file: file.read(n) # Read n characters file.readline() # Read one line file.readlines() # Read all lines Note \n represents a newline. \r represents a carriage return. A collection is an aggregate object that stores multiple pieces of data in a single variable.*Insertion ordered in modern Python (3.7+).Lists are: ordered indexable mutable able to store mixed data types commonly nested to represent 2D and 3D data Common methods: append() pop() insert() remove() reversed() my_list = ["a", "b", "c", "d"] print(my_list[2]) # C my_list.append("e") print(my_list) zeros = 100 * [0] zeros = [0 for _ in range(100)] numbers = list(range(100)) Tuples are: ordered indexable immutable frequently used for returning multiple values from functions def getAccelXYZ(): return (x, y, z) accel = getAccelXYZ() x, y, z = getAccelXYZ() Python also allows swapping values without a temporary variable.x, y = y, x Dictionaries: are indexed by key require unique keys require immutable keys associate every key with a value my_dict = { "one": 1, "two": 2, "three": 3, } my_dict["four"] = 4 Sets are: mutable unordered not indexable intended primarily for membership testing automatically eliminate duplicates char_in = input() if char_in in {"Y", "y", "yes"}: pass bytes objects: store compact binary data are immutable commonly represent ASCII characters with serial.Serial("COM4", baudrate=115200) as ser: print(ser.read(1)) Typical output:b"y" The leading b indicates a bytes object.bytearray behaves similarly but is mutable.ASCII represents characters as integer byte values.Examples:char = ser.read(1) if char.decode() == "G": ... if char[0] == 71: ... if char == b"G": ... All three expressions test for the same character.Unlike lists, arrays store only a single numeric type.Advantages: compact storage predictable memory usage efficient numeric operations Example:import array my_array = array.array("H", 1000 * [0]) Type code "H" creates an unsigned 16-bit integer array. TODO (Instructor Review) The original lecture notes state "No doubles on STM32 Nucleo." It may be worth clarifying that the target hardware has single-precision floating-point hardware while double-precision operations are software-emulated. Prefer with when opening files. Python provides several built-in collection types, each optimized for different use cases. Choose the collection based on mutability, ordering, indexing, and storage efficiency. Binary communication in embedded systems frequently uses bytes and bytearray. array.array is preferable to list when storing large homogeneous numeric datasets.
Lecture 1 - Philosophy of Mechatronics and Python Fundamentals
Object-Oriented Programming
Finite State Machines
MicroPython
Real-Time Systems

lecture-notes/lecture_03_lab_hardware_ov.title: Lecture 3 - Lab Hardware Overview and Toolchain
lecture-notes/lecture_03_lab_hardware_ov.description:
Before writing meaningful firmware, it is important to understand the development hardware, the supporting accessories, and the software tools that will be used throughout the course. Candidate static notes [[STM32 Nucleo]] [[Shoe of Brian]] [UART] [[Virtual COM Port]] [[MicroPython Development Environment]] The primary development board for this course is the STM32 Nucleo-L476RG.
Important components include: U5: Main STM32L476RG microcontroller U2: ST-LINK microcontroller (used for programming/debugging) ST-LINK USB connector (firmware programming) Arduino-compatible headers Morpho expansion headers B1: User button (PC13) LD2: User LED (PA5) Reset button The ST-LINK section and the application MCU are separate devices. The reset button should generally only be used when necessary during development.
The Shoe of Brian expansion board provides: Native USB connector for MicroPython Access to otherwise inaccessible Morpho pins Convenient breakout connections for laboratory hardware The intent is to simplify laboratory wiring while exposing additional I/O.Although this course uses the Nucleo platform, MicroPython also supports many other STM32 boards.Examples shown in lecture include: Official PyBoard (STM32F405)
Native USB SD card slot STM32 Black Pill (STM32F411)
These illustrate that MicroPython is portable across many STM32 devices.Several editors and development environments are suitable.Examples include: Spyder PyCharm Visual Studio Code Advantages: syntax highlighting linting local interpreter debugger optional serial monitor support A lightweight IDE designed specifically for Python and MicroPython.Provides: local interpreter built-in serial monitor simple interface Examples: Notepad++ other code editors Useful for editing source files, but they generally lack debugging and interpreter support.Useful for homework and analysis.Advantages: executable cells embedded equations plots formatted documentation Not recommended for firmware development.Examples: PuTTY screen HyperTerminal many others For this course you will need: A Python editor or IDE (Spyder, PyCharm, or VS Code recommended) A serial terminal (PuTTY recommended) Jupyter Notebook/Lab for homework assignments The course hardware exposes two independent USB connections:
PC ├── USB #1 │ │ │ ST-LINK USB │ │ │ ST-LINK MCU │ │ │ UART │ │ │ Main MCU │ │ └── USB #2 │ Native USB │ Main MCU The ST-LINK interface communicates with the application MCU over UART, while the native USB port connects directly to the application MCU.Universal Asynchronous Receiver Transmitter A true serial communication peripheral. Transfers binary data over TX and RX lines. Operates at a specified baud rate (commonly 115200 baud). TX ---> RX RX <--- TX A communication endpoint presented by the operating system for serial devices.Historically associated with RS-232 hardware, but the abstraction remains in modern operating systems.A USB device that emulates a traditional serial COM port.Many USB devices, including the ST-LINK and many Bluetooth devices, present themselves as virtual COM ports. Become familiar with the physical layout of the Nucleo board. Understand the role of the Shoe of Brian expansion board. Install appropriate development tools before beginning the labs. Distinguish between UART, COM ports, and Virtual COM Ports. Recognize that the board exposes two different USB communication paths. Editorial Note The annotated board photographs are an important part of the lecture. If possible, preserve them alongside the Markdown rather than replacing them with text-only descriptions.
Lecture 2 - Collections and File I/O

lecture-notes/lecture_04_fsms_and_state_.title: Lecture 4 - Finite State Machines and State Transition Diagrams
lecture-notes/lecture_04_fsms_and_state_.description:
Embedded firmware must be deterministic and robust. Throughout this course we will build firmware as a collection of cooperative tasks implemented as finite state machines (FSMs). Candidate static notes [[Finite State Machine]] [[State Transition Diagram]] [[Cooperative Multitasking]] [[Round Robin Scheduling]] [Priority Scheduling] In this course, embedded refers to firmware executing on hardware permanently or semi-permanently embedded within a product, almost always a microcontroller.Python (MicroPython) is used in ME 405 because it provides a productive learning environment while teaching concepts that transfer directly to languages such as C++ and Rust.On a single-core microcontroller only one task executes at any instant. Multitasking is achieved by switching between tasks.Two common approaches are: Preemptive multitasking Cooperative multitasking Not covered in ME 405.Characteristics include: Typically requires an RTOS. Tasks are scheduled according to priority. Higher-priority tasks may interrupt lower-priority tasks. Shared hardware resources become more difficult to manage. In ME 405 every cooperative task is designed as a finite state machine.TaskA portion of code that executes asynchronously with respect to other tasks.State
A mutually exclusive operating mode of a task.Only one state is active at a time.Transition
A change from one state to another.Every transition has: a trigger or condition, an optional transition action, a destination state. Transitions leaving a common state must be mutually exclusive so that exactly one transition is selected.Finite state machines can be viewed as a discrete abstraction of the broader class of state-determined systems encountered in dynamics.A state transition diagram is the graphical representation of a finite state machine.
General conventions: States are drawn as ellipses. Transitions are directed arrows. Every FSM has an explicit start transition. Transition labels contain the condition and, optionally, the transition action. Stateflow (Simulink) is an example of software capable of implementing programs directly from state transition diagrams.One of the most common design questions is deciding whether behavior belongs inside a state or on a transition.Remember: Transitions are effectively instantaneous. Consequently, transition actions should be "one-shot" operations.A simple model might contain only: Open Closed with Opening and Closing represented as transition actions.A higher-fidelity model might instead define four states: Open Opening Closing Closed The appropriate level of detail depends on the goals of the design.Self-transitions are optional. Include them when they improve clarity; omit them when they merely clutter the diagram.Round-robin scheduling executes each task sequentially in a repeating pattern.
The lecture compares three cases with different task periods and execution times to illustrate how timing margin decreases as execution time increases.Priority scheduling executes whichever ready task has the highest assigned priority.
Compared with round-robin scheduling, priority scheduling generally provides improved latency for critical tasks but introduces additional scheduling complexity.
The lecture concludes with a finite state machine programming example.Source:import time S0_INIT = 0 S1_RUN = 1 S2_RUN_THRICE = 2 def main(): # A variable to indicate what state the FSM # is about to run state = 0 # A counter variable used to track runs through # state 2 count = 0 while(True): try: # Implement FSM inside while loop if (state == S0_INIT): # Run state zero code print("The state is ", state) state = S1_RUN elif (state == S1_RUN): # Run state one code print("The state is ", state) state = S2_RUN_THRICE elif (state == S2_RUN_THRICE): # Run state zero code print("The state is ", state) if (count == 2): state = S1_RUN count = 0 else: count += 1 # Increment count else: # If the state isnt 0, 1, or 2 we have an # invalid state raise ValueError('Invalid state') # Sleeping (delaying) for 1/2 second to slow down # the printing time.sleep(0.5) # Trying to catch the "Ctrl-C" keystroke to break out # of the program cleanly except KeyboardInterrupt: break # Once the program is over, do any sort of cleanup as needed print('Program terminated') # THe following block prevents main() from running when the file # is imported instead of run as a main program if __name__ == '__main__': main() Run in your browser:
https://onlinegdb.com/xsR2ikth3 Embedded firmware must be deterministic. ME 405 uses cooperative multitasking implemented with finite state machines. State transition diagrams communicate FSM behavior visually. Keep transition actions instantaneous. Scheduling policy directly affects task latency and determinism.
Lecture 3 - Lab Hardware Overview and Toolchain

insights/observer.html.title: Observer Intuition
insights/observer.html.description: See State FeedbackThe separation principle still holds when using an augmented-state observer that estimates disturbances.For a plantwith a constant disturbance modeldefine the augmented stateAn observer can estimate both plant states and disturbance:The closed-loop poles are the union of: Controller poles of the augmented system Observer poles of the augmented system The controller and observer may therefore still be designed independently.A key realization:The observer's job is estimation, not compensation.The observer estimatesbut does not apply any control action.Compensation occurs only when the controller uses that estimate:For example,The most important insight from the discussion:The observer is not trying to driveorInstead it is trying to driveto zero.The observer dynamics are fundamentally estimation-error dynamics.A perfect observer converges towhich is generally nonzero.SupposeandSubstituting:The plant no longer sees the full disturbance.It sees only the residual disturbanceIfthe disturbance disappears from the plant dynamics.This does not mean the observer has lost track of the disturbance.It means the disturbance has been perfectly compensated.Integral control introducesA disturbance observer introducesBoth are mechanisms for learning the steady-state bias required to reject a constant disturbance.Integral action learns it indirectly through accumulated tracking error.Disturbance estimation learns it directly through model mismatch.Both ultimately generate the control bias needed to satisfyBoth states can contribute a steady-state control bias:This can create the impression that there must be hidden dynamics.The important distinction: Similar purpose does not imply identical dynamics. Similar purpose does not imply zero dynamics. The integrator and disturbance estimate are generally driven by different signals:vs.Therefore they usually evolve differently.Only special choices of gains and structure would make them dynamically equivalent.The redundancy intuition is closer to pole-zero cancellation than to classical zero dynamics.If two controller states learn the same bias term and evolve identically, one state direction can become effectively invisible to the plant.This resembles: Non-minimal realizations Pole-zero cancellation Hidden internal modes However, this only occurs under special circumstances.In general, the states remain dynamically distinct.Plant states:Plant order = 2Observer states:Observer order = 3Total without integrator:Adding integral control:Using disturbance feedback instead of integral action:Thus disturbance feedback can reduce controller order by one state in this implementation.A major practical insight:The disturbance estimate is generated from observer innovation:If measurements are quantized, thencontains: True disturbances Model mismatch Quantization artifacts Thereforemay be highly active and noisy.Using disturbance feedback creates a direct path:Measurement noise → Observer → Disturbance estimate → Control inputwhich can inject noise into the actuator.The integrator evolves according toAn integrator naturally accumulates persistent bias and tends to average out high-frequency quantization effects.Therefore: Disturbance feedback may react to quantization artifacts. Integral action primarily reacts to long-term offset. In systems where the disturbance state is being used by the observer to absorb measurement quantization or model mismatch, integral action may produce cleaner control behavior despite appearing theoretically redundant.A disturbance observer and an integrator both provide mechanisms for rejecting constant disturbances.The disturbance observer: Learns the disturbance explicitly. Can provide faster compensation. Can reduce controller order if an augmented observer already exists. May reintroduce measurement noise through the disturbance feedback path. Integral action: Learns the required compensation indirectly through accumulated error. Is highly robust to modeling errors. Naturally suppresses high-frequency measurement artifacts. Often produces cleaner behavior when disturbance estimates are noisy. For systems with significant quantization or observer-induced disturbance-estimate noise, integral action may be preferable even when a disturbance estimate is available.

headers.heading: Disturbance Observers vs Integral Action — Key Insights
1. Separation Principle with Augmented Disturbance States
2. The Observer Does Not Compensate Disturbances
3. The Important Quantity Is Estimation Error
4. Why Disturbance Compensation Does Not Hide the Disturbance
5. Integral Action and Disturbance Estimation Solve Similar Problems
6. Why Integral Action and Disturbance Feedback Can Feel Redundant
8. State Counting for the Motor Example
9. Why Integral Action May Still Be Better
10. Integral Action Naturally Filters Quantization Effects

info_showCustomIcons.description: Show custom icons for files and folders

info_showDefaultFolderIcons.description: Show a default icon of a folder for every folder in the tree

info_showDefaultFileIcons.description: Show a default icon of a file for every file in the tree

info_defaultFolderIcon.description: The icon to use for folders. Prefix with 'lucide//' to use a Lucide icon

info_defaultFileIcon.description: The icon to use for files. Prefix with 'lucide//' to use a Lucide icon

info_defaultMediaIcon.description: The icon to use for media files. Prefix with 'lucide//' to use a Lucide icon

info_exposeStartingPath.description: Whether or not to show the current file in the file tree when the page is first loaded

info_showOrphanNodes.description: Show nodes that are not connected to any other nodes.

info_showAttachments.description: Show attachments like images and PDFs as nodes in the graph.

info_allowGlobalGraph.description: Allow the user to view the global graph of all nodes.

info_allowExpand.description: Allow the user to pop-out the graph view to take up the whole screen

info_attractionForce.description: How much should linked nodes attract each other? This will make the graph appear more clustered.

info_linkLength.description: How long should the links between nodes be? The shorter the links the more connected nodes will cluster together.

info_repulsionForce.description: How much should nodes repel each other? This will make disconnected parts more spread out.

info_centralForce.description: How much should nodes be attracted to the center? This will make the graph appear more dense and circular.

info_edgePruning.description: Edges with a length above this threshold will not be rendered, however they will still contribute to the simulation. This can help large tangled graphs look more organised. Hovering over a node will still display these links.

info_minNodeRadius.description: How small should the smallest nodes be? The smaller a node is the less it will attract other nodes.

info_maxNodeRadius.description: How large should the largest nodes be? Nodes are sized by how many links they have. The larger a node is the more it will attract other nodes. This can be used to create a good grouping around the most important nodes.

info_allowResizing.description: Whether or not to allow the sidebars to be resized

info_allowCollapsing.description: Whether or not to allow the sidebars to be collapsed

info_rightDefaultWidth.description: The default width of the right sidebar

info_leftDefaultWidth.description: The default width of the left sidebar

info_selector.description: CSS selector for an element. The feature will be placed relative to this element.

info_type.description: Will this feature be placed before, after, or inside (at the beggining or end).

info_featurePlacement.description: Where to place this feature on the page. (Relative to the selector)

info_sourcePath.description: The local path to the source .html file which will be included.

info_allowFoldingLists.description: Whether or not to allow lists to be folded

info_allowFoldingHeadings.description: Whether or not to allow headings to be folded

info_siteUrl.description: The url that this site will be hosted at

info_authorName.description: The name of the author of the site
